headline: Paris Accelerates Climate Action, Prioritizing Bikes and Pedestrians Under Mayor Hidalgo
deck: As Mayor Anne Hidalgo concludes her 12-year tenure, Paris stands as a global example of urban greening—achieved through ambitious policy shifts and despite significant motorist opposition.
body: Paris has undergone a profound urban transformation under Mayor Anne Hidalgo, dramatically shifting away from car-centric infrastructure to prioritize cycling, pedestrian zones, and public green spaces. This ambitious overhaul, implemented over Hidalgo's 12-year tenure, is reshaping how residents navigate and experience the French capital.

Since Hidalgo became Mayor in 2014, Paris has planted 155,000 trees, added several hundred kilometers of segregated bike lanes, and pedestrianized 300 school streets. These changes have fundamentally altered the city’s landscape and daily commute.

The impact is visible in the experiences of residents like Corentin Roudaut, an IT developer who moved to Paris 10 years ago. He recalls being "scared to cycle" due to the heavy car presence and a distinct lack of protection for cyclists.

A pivotal moment for Roudaut came with the creation of a segregated bike lane on Boulevard Voltaire in the 11th arrondissement. This dedicated infrastructure provided the safety and encouragement needed for him to return to cycling.

Roudaut now volunteers with Paris en Selle, a prominent cycling campaign group, advocating for further improvements. He notes the progressive nature of the city's shift, stating, “It was a process that started slow and really accelerated in the last 10 years.”

Beyond bike lanes, Hidalgo's administration banned cars from the banks of the Seine, transforming former roadways into public promenades. Parking spots across the city have also been converted into green spaces and cafe terraces, further reclaiming urban land for residents.
Mayor Hidalgo reflects on the generational impact of these changes. She states, “Today there are generations of children who have not known cars there. People say ‘wow!’ when you tell them.”

This extensive transformation has not been without resistance. Motorists have consistently opposed the changes, with political figures like Rachida Dati describing the new urban landscape as “anxiety-inducing.”

Referendums on policies like charging SUV drivers more for parking and the pedestrianization of school streets passed, but often with low turnouts, highlighting the persistent divisions within the city. Hidalgo herself acknowledged the difficulty, calling it “a tough battle.”

Experts attribute Paris's ability to implement such ambitious changes partly to its tight administrative boundaries and the groundwork laid by previous mayoral administrations. This established foundation facilitated the rapid urban transition.

Paris's efforts to become a more livable and sustainable city are now held up as a leading example for progressive European cities and beyond. Corentin Roudaut offers advice to other urban planners: “When people ask me if I have any advice, I say don’t be afraid of being ambitious.”

The city's success resonates globally, with Roudaut observing, “At least in some parts of the city, we have a [cycle] network that is starting to be safe and pretty much complete.” He adds, “everybody’s saying: ‘Look at what Paris has done, it’s so amazing.’”
sources: How Paris swapped cars for bikes – and transformed its streets

pipelineSteps.name: Source Discovery
